shower.thought: We spend the first part of our lives learning to speak, and the rest of our lives learning what not to say.
You never actually see your own face—you've only ever seen reversed versions of it and photographs taken by other people.
The people who say they don't care what others think are usually the ones who care the most, just about different people.
Nostalgia is just grief wearing a smile.
You can't remember most of your life, which means you're basically a different person every few years who inherited the previous person's problems.
Every conversation you have with a stranger is just two lonely people trying to prove they exist.
We assume quiet people are thinking deep thoughts, but they might just have nothing to say—and we'll never know which.
The reason we feel so tired isn't always because we haven't slept enough; sometimes it's because we haven't rested our minds in weeks.
You probably won't remember the moment you stopped playing pretend, just like you don't remember the moment you started.
Money is just a collectively agreed-upon delusion that we all decided to believe in so we could stop bartering chickens.
The hardest part about being honest is that nobody ever asks you to be—they ask you to be nice instead.
We're all just trying to figure out if we're the main character or an NPC in everyone else's story.
Your childhood pet didn't know it was your childhood—it was just its whole life.
The moment you understand what your parents did wrong is the same moment you realize you'll probably do the same things to your own kids.
We call them "bad memories" but they're just the ones we think about the most.
Time moves faster as you get older because each year becomes a smaller percentage of your total life—and that's also depressing.
You can't taste your own saliva, which means part of your mouth is permanently a stranger to you.
The people who hurt you the most are usually the ones who remind you the most of yourself.
We spend so much time trying to be ourselves that we never stop to ask if we even know what that means.
Your phone has probably forgotten more things about you than you remember about yourself.

motivational.thought: The man who moves a mountain begins by carrying away small stones. — Confucius
Regret is the tax you pay for waiting. — Unknown
Your worst day of execution beats your best day of planning. — Unknown
The gap between who you are and who you want to be is filled only with action. — Unknown
What we resist persists. What we accept transforms. — Carl Jung
You're not behind. You're not ahead. You're exactly where your choices have taken you. — Unknown
The person you'll be in five years depends on two things: the books you read and the people you surround yourself with. — Unknown
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most. — Unknown
Small improvements over time create remarkable results that seem to happen overnight. — Unknown
The only permission you need is the kind you give yourself. — Unknown
Bitterness is like drinking poison and expecting someone else to suffer. — Buddha
Your obstacles are not stopping you—your belief about them is. — Unknown
Every expert was once a beginner who refused to quit. — Unknown
If you're waiting for the right time, you've already decided not to do it. — Unknown
The cost of staying the same will eventually exceed the cost of change. — Unknown
You can't outrun your own mind. So make peace with it first. — Unknown
What we call luck is usually just preparation meeting opportunity. — Unknown
The person in the arena is the only one whose opinion matters. — Theodore Roosevelt
Your life is not a problem to be solved; it's a rhythm to be danced. — Unknown
Comparison kills gratitude, and gratitude kills despair. — Unknown

darkjoke.thought:
My wife asked if I believed in life after death.
I said, "I don't know, but I'm pretty sure you will."

I told my boss I was going through a existential crisis.
He said, "Take it up with HR." I said, "They're the ones causing it."

My therapist asked what my biggest fear was.
I said, "Becoming like you." She said, "You already are—you just haven't realized it yet."

I went to a self-help seminar on overcoming grief.
Turns out I was the only one who showed up. The instructor never came either.

My dad said, "Son, one day you'll understand why I left."
I said, "Yeah, after I have kids."

I asked my friend how his new antidepressants were working.
He said, "Great! I don't care about anything anymore."
I said, "So they're working."

My gym trainer said, "No pain, no gain."
I said, "What if I'm paying for both?"

I realized I'm addicted to irony.
Which is ironic because I never thought that would happen.

The suicide hotline kept putting me on hold.
I said, "If I wanted to wait this long, I'd just keep living."

I asked my accountant if I could write off my funeral expenses.
He said, "Only if you use them."

My wife said she was leaving me because I never listen.
At least that's what I think she said; I wasn't really paying attention.

I told my doctor my memory was getting worse.
He said, "When did this start?"
I said, "When did what start?"

My LinkedIn profile says I'm "between opportunities."
My actual status is "between creditors."

I went to my high school reunion.
Everyone asked what I'd been up to. I said, "Mostly avoiding you guys."

I asked the funeral director what happens to unclaimed bodies.
He said, "Eventually someone claims them."
I said, "Good, I was worried I'd end up with mine."

My mom asked why I never call.
I said, "I didn't want to interrupt your new family."
She said, "That was ten years ago." I said, "Exactly."

My coworker said she was thinking about going back to school.
I said, "You never left, you're just really slow."

I told my psychiatrist I had a fear of commitment.
He said we should probably stop seeing each other then.

My friend said he was living his best life.
I checked his Instagram—he'd gained forty pounds and gotten divorced.
I guess we have different metrics.

I asked my dad why he and mom never divorced.
He said, "It was cheaper to stay married and suffer than to split and pay alimony."
I said, "That's the most romantic thing I've ever heard."
